# Results

We evaluated the pipeline across three hematology/oncology case studies selected to represent distinct clinical landscapes: hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis (HLH), diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL), and multiple myeloma (MM). These topics were chosen deliberately because they differ along several axes that stress-test a general-purpose automation pipeline. HLH is a rare, heterogeneous syndrome where evidence is dominated by case series and retrospective cohorts rather than randomized trials, demanding that the extraction module handle sparse and inconsistently reported data [@Hsu2026HLH]. DLBCL represents a rapidly evolving treatment landscape with recent approvals in bispecific antibodies and CAR-T cell therapy, requiring the search strategy to capture emerging evidence alongside established chemoimmunotherapy regimens [@Ansell2026DLBCL]. Multiple myeloma offers the deepest evidence base of the three, with dozens of randomized controlled trials supporting a complex, sequentially-deployed drug armamentarium, testing the pipeline's ability to synthesize dense quantitative data across treatment lines [@Dimopoulos2021IMWG]. Together, the three topics span rare-to-common diseases, narrative-to-quantitative evidence structures, and stable-to-rapidly-evolving therapeutic landscapes.

## Pipeline performance metrics

Table 1 summarizes the end-to-end performance of the pipeline across all three case studies. Each run began with an automated multi-database search across Scopus, PubMed, and Embase, followed by deduplication, journal quality filtering, and DOI validation against the Crossref API.

| Metric | HLH | DLBCL | Multiple Myeloma |
|--------|-----|-------|-----------------|
| Records identified | 897 | 500 | 499 |
| After deduplication | 783 | 500 | 499 |
| Articles included | 50 | 50 | 50 |
| Abstracts retrieved | 47/50 (94%) | 49/50 (98%) | 47/50 (94%) |
| DOIs validated | 50/50 (100%) | 49/50 (98%) | 50/50 (100%) |
| Unique journals | 32 | 26 | 20 |
| Q1 journals | 29 (91%) | --- | --- |
| Word count | 12,755 | 10,636 | 10,934 |
| Unique citations | 48 | ~45 | ~45 |
| PRISMA score | 35/36 | 35/36 | 33/36 |
| Render warnings | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| Approx. pipeline time | ~25 min | ~20 min | ~20 min |

: Pipeline performance metrics across three hematology/oncology case studies. {#tbl-pipeline}

The HLH case study retrieved the largest initial record set (897), reflecting broader search terms needed for a syndrome with heterogeneous nomenclature; deduplication removed 114 records (12.7%). The DLBCL and MM searches returned approximately 500 records each with negligible duplication, consistent with more standardized terminology in these diseases. All three runs converged on 50 included articles after LLM-assisted screening and human-in-the-loop confirmation. Abstract retrieval rates ranged from 94% to 98%, with missing abstracts attributable to conference proceedings and older publications lacking structured metadata in PubMed. DOI validation was at or near 100% in all cases, confirming the reliability of the Crossref API verification step.

The generated manuscripts ranged from 10,636 to 12,755 words, appropriate for journal-length review articles. Each manuscript rendered without warnings in Quarto, indicating well-formed Markdown and citation syntax. Approximate pipeline execution times of 20--25 minutes represent a reduction of several orders of magnitude compared with the 6--18 months reported for conventional systematic reviews [@Borah2017Analysis], though the pipeline time excludes human-in-the-loop review intervals, which in practice added 1--3 hours per case study.

## Structured extraction quality

A key innovation of the pipeline is the use of Claude Haiku with field-level schema enforcement for structured data extraction, replacing the regular-expression (regex) baseline used in earlier iterations. We evaluated both approaches on the HLH case study, where evidence heterogeneity poses the greatest extraction challenge. Table 2 presents the comparison.

| Metric | Regex | Haiku LLM | Improvement |
|--------|-------|-----------|-------------|
| Articles with quantitative data | 21/50 (42%) | 35/50 (70%) | +67% |
| Average richness score (/10) | 1.2 | 2.2 | +83% |
| Study type identified | 0% | 86% | New capability |
| Sample size extracted | 16% | 56% | +250% |
| Drug dosing captured | 12% | 48% | +300% |
| p-values captured | 8% | 36% | +350% |

: Structured extraction comparison between regex baseline and Haiku LLM extractor (HLH case study). {#tbl-extraction}

The Haiku-based extractor identified quantitative data in 70% of included articles, compared with 42% for the regex approach---a 67% relative improvement. The average richness score, defined as the number of distinct structured fields populated per article on a 10-point scale, increased from 1.2 to 2.2. Critically, the LLM extractor enabled entirely new capabilities that regex patterns could not address: study type classification (achieved in 86% of articles) and nuanced drug dosing capture (48% vs. 12%). The largest relative gains appeared in p-value extraction (+350%), reflecting the LLM's ability to parse heterogeneous statistical reporting formats---inline, tabular, and parenthetical---that defeat fixed regex patterns. These improvements translate directly into richer evidence tables in the generated manuscripts, enabling more granular narrative synthesis of treatment outcomes and effect sizes.

## PRISMA 2020 compliance

The pipeline includes an automated PRISMA 2020 self-audit module that evaluates all 27 checklist items (expanded to 36 sub-items per the PRISMA 2020 reporting standard) and triggers repair loops for non-compliant elements [@Page2021PRISMA]. Table 3 presents PRISMA compliance scores by section across the three case studies.

| PRISMA section | Max sub-items | HLH | DLBCL | MM |
|----------------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Title | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Abstract | 1 | 1 | 1 | 1 |
| Introduction | 2 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| Methods | 15 | 15 | 15 | 14 |
| Results | 10 | 10 | 10 | 9 |
| Discussion | 4 | 4 | 4 | 4 |
| Other information | 3 | 2 | 2 | 2 |
| **Total** | **36** | **35** | **35** | **33** |

: PRISMA 2020 compliance scores by section across three case studies. {#tbl-prisma}

All three manuscripts achieved scores above 90% (91.7%--97.2%). The most consistently missed sub-item was 13f (sensitivity analysis), which is not applicable to narrative synthesis reviews that do not perform meta-analysis; this item was scored as not satisfied rather than not applicable to provide a conservative estimate. In the MM case study, two additional sub-items in the Methods and Results sections received partial credit due to keyword-threshold matching in the audit module rather than substantive content gaps---manual review confirmed that the underlying text addressed the relevant PRISMA requirements. No manuscript failed any sub-item related to search strategy documentation, inclusion criteria specification, or risk-of-bias reporting, which represent the most methodologically consequential elements of the PRISMA checklist.

## Comparison with expert-written reviews

To assess whether pipeline-generated manuscripts achieve clinical content comparable to expert-authored reviews, we compared the HLH output with the independently published review by Hsu et al. [@Hsu2026HLH], a comprehensive narrative review of HLH in adults published in *Blood*. Table 4 summarizes the comparison across key content and quality dimensions.

| Feature | Hsu et al. [@Hsu2026HLH] | Pipeline (HLH) |
|---------|--------------------------|-----------------|
| Word count | ~8,000 | 12,755 |
| References cited | ~120 | 48 |
| Diagnostic criteria specificity | HLH-2004, HScore detailed | HLH-2004, HScore referenced |
| Treatment dosing detail | Etoposide, ruxolitinib, emapalumab doses | Etoposide, ruxolitinib, emapalumab doses |
| Organism-specific guidance | EBV, Leishmania, HIV discussed | EBV, Leishmania, HIV discussed |
| Clinical trial data | DEP, Emapalumab pivotal trial | DEP, Emapalumab pivotal trial |
| Epidemiology coverage | Incidence, mortality, age distribution | Incidence, mortality, trigger distribution |
| PRISMA 2020 checklist | Not applicable (narrative review) | 35/36 sub-items satisfied |
| Estimated production time | Months (expert effort) | ~25 min pipeline + ~2 h HITL |

: Content comparison between expert-written review and pipeline-generated manuscript for HLH. {#tbl-expert}

The pipeline-generated manuscript was longer (12,755 vs. ~8,000 words) but cited fewer references (48 vs. ~120), reflecting the 50-article inclusion cap imposed by the current pipeline configuration. Despite this constraint, the pipeline manuscript covered the same diagnostic criteria (HLH-2004, HScore), the same key therapeutic agents with comparable dosing specificity, and the same organism-specific management considerations. Both reviews discussed the pivotal DEP regimen and emapalumab trial data. The expert review provided deeper mechanistic discussion of pathophysiology and more extensive coverage of secondary HLH etiologies, areas where the pipeline's reliance on structured extraction from abstracts and available full-text limits depth. Conversely, the pipeline manuscript provided a complete PRISMA-compliant reporting framework that the expert narrative review, by design, did not include. These results suggest that the pipeline achieves clinically meaningful content parity for treatment-focused reviews while adding systematic methodology and reproducibility guarantees.

## Comparison with existing tools

Finally, we compared the pipeline's feature set against established systematic review automation tools, including ASReview [@vandeSchoot2021ASReview], Rayyan [@Ouzzani2016Rayyan], otto-SR [@ottoSR2025], LitLLM [@LitLLM2024], and Covidence [@Covidence2024]. Table 5 presents a feature matrix across 11 capability dimensions relevant to end-to-end systematic review production.

| Feature | ASReview | Rayyan | otto-SR | LitLLM | Covidence | Ours |
|---------|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Multi-database search | --- | --- | Partial | --- | --- | Yes |
| LLM-assisted screening | --- | --- | Yes | Yes | --- | Yes |
| Structured data extraction | --- | --- | Partial | Partial | Manual | Yes |
| Full manuscript generation | --- | --- | Draft | Yes | --- | Yes |
| PRISMA 2020 self-audit | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | Yes |
| DOI validation (Crossref) | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | Yes |
| Journal quality filtering | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | Yes |
| Human-in-the-loop checkpoints | --- | Yes | --- | --- | Yes | Yes |
| Cover letter generation | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | Yes |
| Open source | Yes | --- | Yes | Yes | --- | Yes |
| CI/CD integration | Yes | --- | Yes | --- | --- | Yes |

: Feature comparison across systematic review automation tools. Yes = fully implemented; Partial = limited implementation; --- = not available. {#tbl-tools}

ASReview and Rayyan focus exclusively on the screening phase: ASReview uses active learning to prioritize records for human review, while Rayyan provides a collaborative web interface for title-and-abstract screening [@vandeSchoot2021ASReview; @Ouzzani2016Rayyan]. Neither tool performs data extraction, synthesis, or manuscript generation. Covidence extends further into the workflow with manual extraction templates and risk-of-bias tools but does not automate writing or reporting [@Covidence2024]. Otto-SR and LitLLM represent the most directly comparable tools, as both employ LLMs for screening and manuscript drafting [@ottoSR2025; @LitLLM2024]. However, neither implements PRISMA self-audit with automated repair, DOI validation against an external API, or journal quality filtering---features that address the verifiability and methodological rigor concerns identified as barriers to adoption of AI-assisted systematic reviews [@Ge2024Leveraging; @Khalil2024Automation].

Our pipeline is the only tool in this comparison that spans the complete workflow from multi-database search through journal-formatted manuscript with embedded quality assurance. The combination of PRISMA self-audit, DOI validation, and nine human-in-the-loop checkpoints distinguishes the approach from fully automated tools that prioritize speed over verifiability, while the open-source release and CI/CD integration support reproducibility and community contribution.
